name: Suspicious PowerShell
title: Encoded PowerShell execution on {host} by {user}
description: PowerShell launched with an encoded command and download-style arguments on {host}, spawned by an Office application. This pattern is consistent with a malicious document macro.
objective: Determine why PowerShell ran with suspicious arguments on {host}, identify the parent process chain, and decide whether this is malicious execution or legitimate administration.
checklist: Run the detection query and locate the PowerShell process-creation event
Examine the command line for red flags (encoded commands, hidden window, download strings)
Identify the parent process — what launched PowerShell?
Check for follow-on activity: network connections or DNS queries from {host} right after execution
Check whether {user} is an administrator who routinely runs PowerShell
Disposition the case and recommend host isolation if malicious
hints: Look at the ParentImage field in the sysmon process event — PowerShell spawned by winword.exe is almost never legitimate.
The flags -enc / -w hidden / -nop are classic malicious tradecraft; admins rarely combine them.
Check sysmon EventCode 22 (DNS) from the same host in the minutes after execution — the script reached out to a suspicious domain.
expectedEvidence: PowerShell process creation with encoded/hidden arguments
Parent process is winword.exe (Office spawning a shell)
DNS query to a suspicious domain immediately after execution
No change-management record or admin justification
explanation: This is malicious execution consistent with a phishing document macro. Three independent signals converge: (1) the parent chain — winword.exe spawning powershell.exe is a top-tier detection because documents shouldn't launch shells; (2) the argument pattern — '-nop -w hidden -enc' exists to evade logging and the user's notice; (3) immediate outbound DNS to a low-reputation domain indicates a stager fetching a payload. Legitimate admin PowerShell is typically interactive, unencoded, and parented by explorer.exe or terminal apps. Correct response: isolate the host, escalate, and pull the email that delivered the document.
reportTemplate: 1. Isolate {host} from the network. 2. Retrieve and detonate the source document in a sandbox. 3. Block the callback domain {dga_domain}. 4. Search for the same document hash across the fleet. 5. Reset credentials for {user}.

events.message: Process Create: Image: C:\Program Files\Microsoft Office\winword.exe CommandLine: winword.exe /n "C:\Users\{user}\Downloads\Invoice_Q3_2026.docm" ParentImage: C:\Windows\explorer.exe

Process Create: Image: C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe CommandLine: powershell.exe -nop -w hidden -enc SQBFAFgAIAAoAE4AZQB3AC0ATwBiAGoAZQBjAHQA... ParentImage: C:\Program Files\Microsoft Office\winword.exe
action=allowed proto=tcp src={src_ip} dst={c2_ip} dport=443 bytes_out=2841
